skill.name: app-builder
skill.description: Application building orchestrator that turns natural language requests into project plans, stack choices, scaffolding guidance, and coordinated agent workflows.
skill.summary: Plan, scaffold, and coordinate full-stack application builds from natural language requests.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 60 issues, but review found the command, weak-crypto, network, and environment hits are Markdown examples, filenames, or ordinary prose. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscated code, or executable payloads were found in the reviewed files. The skill remains medium risk because it requests broad Bash, Write, Edit, and Agent capabilities for application generation.

medium_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions for Application Generation
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash, Write, Edit, and Agent access. This is expected for an app builder, but it can execute project commands, modify files, and delegate work if a user enables it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permissions are explicitly declared on the allowed-tools line. The reviewed content uses them for legitimate application-building workflows, so this is elevated capability risk rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static findings for Ruby or shell backtick execution are false positives. The cited sections are Markdown diagrams, tables, path examples, or configuration examples, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched content is inside Markdown formatting or inline path references. I found no Ruby backtick expression, shell interpolation, or command string executed by the skill files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto findings do not correspond to cryptographic APIs or hash operations. They appear to be triggered by ordinary prose, template names, or Markdown content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptions, table rows, or template names. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic implementation is present in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Environment File References Are Scaffold Filenames
low_findings.description: The environment findings are references to .env.example and .env.local in a proposed project tree. The skill does not read, print, transmit, or request existing secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines list filenames for generated projects and a template purpose. I found no instruction to inspect current environment files or expose secret material.

low_findings.title: Network Library Detection Is Natural Language Text
low_findings.description: The network finding at SKILL.md line 3 is a false positive. The line describes natural language requests, not a Python requests import or outbound network call.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The word request appears in a plain-language skill description. No HTTP library import, URL, fetch call, or exfiltration destination appears in the reviewed files.

content.user_title: Build Full-Stack Apps From Natural Language
content.value_statement: Starting a new application often requires many architecture decisions before useful coding begins. This skill converts a request into a project type, stack choice, scaffold plan, and coordinated build workflow.
actual_capabilities: Classifies project requests using a keyword matrix for apps, APIs, dashboards, SaaS products, and extensions.
Recommends default and alternative technology stacks for modern web, mobile, desktop, API, and CLI projects.
Provides a feature-based Next.js full-stack directory structure with core files and path aliases.
Defines a multi-agent build sequence with planning, backend, frontend, database, testing, and deployment phases.
Guides feature additions by mapping requested changes to database, backend, frontend, dependency, and configuration work.
limitations: The skill is guidance-only and does not include executable scaffold scripts or reusable source templates in the reviewed files.
Stack recommendations may age and should be checked against current framework releases before production use.
Complex applications still need human review for security, data modeling, compliance, and deployment choices.
Generated plans depend on the clarity and completeness of the user's initial request.

use_cases.title: Plan a New SaaS Product
use_cases.description: Turn a product idea into a stack recommendation, feature list, scaffold structure, and staged build plan.

use_cases.title: Coordinate a Multi-Agent Build
use_cases.description: Break a full-stack project into planner, database, backend, frontend, testing, security, and deployment phases.

use_cases.title: Add Features to an Existing App
use_cases.description: Analyze a requested feature and identify the required database, API, UI, dependency, and configuration changes.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Simple App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use app-builder to plan a task manager app with login, projects, tasks, and a simple dashboard.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Stack
prompt_templates.prompt: Use app-builder to classify this product idea and recommend a practical stack: a paid newsletter platform with subscriptions and admin tools.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use app-builder to plan adding Stripe checkout to my existing Next.js app. Include database, backend, frontend, and configuration changes.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate a Full Build
prompt_templates.prompt: Use app-builder to create a phased build plan for a marketplace app, then coordinate planner, database, backend, frontend, test, security, and deployment work.

output_examples.input: Build a CRM for tracking customers, deals, and sales tasks.
output_examples.output: The skill identifies the request as a full-stack CRM, recommends a Next.js full-stack template, outlines customer and deal data models, and sequences planning, backend, frontend, testing, and deployment work.

output_examples.input: Add payment support to my existing app.
output_examples.output: The skill maps the feature to order and payment tables, checkout and webhook endpoints, checkout UI, Stripe dependencies, environment configuration, testing, and recovery steps.

best_practices: Give the skill a clear product goal, target users, core workflows, and required integrations.
Ask for a plan review before allowing file edits, command execution, or agent delegation.
Validate generated architecture, dependencies, environment handling, and security controls before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill as a substitute for security, privacy, or compliance review.
Do not allow broad Bash execution without reviewing the proposed commands first.
Do not accept outdated framework versions or hosted services without checking current project requirements.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps plan and scaffold web apps, SaaS products, APIs, mobile apps, desktop apps, browser extensions, CLIs, and monorepos.

faq.question: Does it create production-ready code by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides orchestration, planning, stack selection, and scaffold guidance. Production code still needs implementation and review.

faq.question: Why does it need command and file permissions?
faq.answer: The skill is designed for app-building workflows that may create files, edit projects, install dependencies, run tests, and start previews.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning. The reviewed static alerts were false positives, but the declared tool permissions are broad.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should users review before running it?
faq.answer: Users should review planned commands, generated files, dependency choices, environment files, and any agent delegation steps.
